episodes.title: Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
episodes.description:
Keith Rabois was an early executive at PayPal (part of the famous PayPal Mafia), COO at Square, VP of Corporate Development at LinkedIn, and an early investor in Stripe, DoorDash, Airbnb, YouTube, Ramp, and Palantir. Currently he’s managing director at Khosla Ventures. Also, he hasn’t touched a computer since September 2010 (he does everything from an iPad).
In our in-depth conversation, Keith shares:
1. The barrels vs. ammunition hiring framework (and how to spot barrels)
2. Why talking to customers is actively harmful for consumer products
3. How to identify undiscovered talent
4. Why the PM role is dying
5. The three traits of the best-performing companies right now
6. The specific interview question he asks every senior candidate
7. Why CMOs (not engineers) are becoming the #1 consumer of tokens
episodes.descriptionCN:
Keith Rabois 是 PayPal（著名的 PayPal Mafia 的一部分）的早期高管、Square 的首席运营官、LinkedIn 的企业发展副总裁，以及 Stripe、DoorDash、Airbnb、YouTube、Ramp 和 Palantir 的早期投资者。目前，他担任科斯拉风险投资公司 (Khosla Ventures) 的董事总经理。此外，自 2010 年 9 月以来，他就没有碰过电脑（他所有的事情都通过 iPad 进行）。
在我们的深入对话中，基思分享了：
1. 枪管与弹药租赁框架（以及如何发现枪管）
2. 为什么与客户交谈对消费品有害
3. 如何识别未被发现的人才
4. 为什么 PM 角色正在消亡
5. 目前表现最好的公司的三个特征
6. 他向每一位资深候选人提出的具体面试问题
7. 为什么 CMO（而不是工程师）正在成为代币的第一大消费者

episodes.title: Head of Growth (Anthropic): “Claude is growing itself at this point” | Amol Avasare
episodes.description:
Amol Avasare is Head of Growth at Anthropic, which is going through the most unprecedented growth trajectory in history—scaling from $1 billion to over $19 billion in ARR in just 14 months. Previously, Amol worked on the growth teams at Mercury and MasterClass. Before that he was a founder, and he cold emailed his way into the Anthropic role when no job listing existed. Most remarkably, he overcame a traumatic brain injury from a Muay Thai match that meant he couldn't work for nearly a year.
In our in-depth discussion, Amol shares:
1. How Amol landed his role by cold emailing Anthropic’s CPO Mike Krieger
2. How Anthropic is automating growth experiments with Claude (their internal tool called “CASH”)
3. Why the ratio of PMs to engineers might need to flip (more PMs than engineers) as AI makes engineers exponentially more productive
4. Why activation is the single highest-leverage growth problem in AI
5. Why Anthropic indexes 70/30 toward big bets (the opposite of most growth teams)
6. How he uses Cowork to detect team misalignment in Slack
7. How the company’s focus on AI coding created a research flywheel that accelerated their models
episodes.descriptionCN:
Amol Avasare 是 Anthropic 的增长主管，该公司正在经历历史上最前所未有的增长轨迹——ARR 在短短 14 个月内从 10 亿美元扩大到超过 190 亿美元。此前，Amol 曾在 Mercury 和 MasterClass 的增长团队工作。在此之前，他是一名创始人，在没有职位空缺的情况下，他通过冷冰冰的电子邮件进入了人类角色。最引人注目的是，他克服了泰拳比赛造成的脑外伤，这意味着他近一年无法工作。
在我们的深入讨论中，Amol 分享了：
1. Amol 是如何通过向 Anthropic 的 CPO Mike Krieger 发送冷邮件而获得职位的
2. Anthropic 如何与 Claude 一起自动化生长实验（他们的内部工具称为“CASH”）
3. 为什么随着人工智能使工程师的工作效率成倍提高，PM 与工程师的比例可能需要调整（PM 的数量多于工程师）
4. 为什么激活是人工智能中杠杆率最高的单一增长问题
5. 为什么 Anthropic 对大赌注的指数为 70/30（与大多数增长团队相反）
6. 他如何使用 Cowork 来检测 Slack 中的团队失调
7. 该公司对人工智能编码的关注如何创建了一个研究飞轮来加速他们的模型

episodes.title: An AI state of the union: We’ve passed the inflection point, dark factories are coming, and automation timelines | Simon Willison
episodes.description:
Simon Willison is a prolific independent software developer, a blogger, and one of the most visible and trusted voices on the impact AI is having on builders. He co-created Django, the web framework that powers Instagram, Pinterest, and tens of thousands of other websites. He coined the term “prompt injection,” popularized the terms “AI slop” and “agentic engineering,” and has built over 100 open source projects, including Datasette, a data analysis tool used by investigative journalists worldwide. What makes Simon unique is that he’s made the leap from traditional software engineering to AI-native development more fully and visibly than almost anyone—and he’s been documenting everything he learns in real time on his blog, SimonWillison.net.
In our in-depth conversation, Simon shares:
1. Why November 2025 was the inflection point when AI coding agents crossed from “mostly works” to “actually works”
2. How Simon writes 95% of his code from his phone now and why he’s mentally exhausted by 11 a.m.
3. Why mid-career engineers (not juniors) are most at risk right now
4. The three agentic engineering patterns Simon uses daily (red/green TDD, templates, hoarding)
5. The next leap: the “dark factory” pattern where nobody writes or reviews code and AI does its own QA
6. Why prompt injection is an unsolved security problem and the “lethal trifecta” that will likely lead to an AI Challenger disaster
7. Why the pelican riding a bicycle became the unofficial benchmark for AI model quality
episodes.descriptionCN:
Simon Willison 是一位多产的独立软件开发人员、博主，也是人工智能对建筑商影响方面最引人注目、最值得信赖的声音之一。他共同创建了 Django，这是一个为 Instagram、Pinterest 和数以万计的其他网站提供支持的 Web 框架。他创造了“即时注入”一词，推广了“AI slop”和“代理工程”等术语，并构建了 100 多个开源项目，其中包括 Datasette，这是一种全球调查记者使用的数据分析工具。 Simon 的独特之处在于，他比几乎任何人都更全面、更明显地实现了从传统软件工程到 AI 原生开发的飞跃，而且他一直在他的博客 SimonWillison.net 上实时记录他学到的一切。
在我们的深入对话中，西蒙分享了：
1. 为什么 2025 年 11 月是 AI 编码代理从“大部分有效”跨越到“实际有效”的拐点
2. Simon 现在 95% 的代码是如何通过手机编写的，以及为什么他到了上午 11 点就精神疲惫。
3. 为什么处于职业生涯中期的工程师（而不是初级工程师）现在面临的风险最大
4. Simon 日常使用的三种代理工程模式（红/绿 TDD、模板、囤积）
5. 下一个飞跃：“黑暗工厂”模式，没有人编写或审查代码，人工智能自己进行质量检查
6. 为什么即时注入是一个未解决的安全问题以及可能导致 AI Challenger 灾难的“致命三连击”
7. 为什么骑自行车的鹈鹕成为AI模型质量的非官方标杆

episodes.title: From skeptic to true believer: How OpenClaw changed my life | Claire Vo
episodes.description:
Claire Vo is the host of our sister podcast, “How I AI,” a former product executive and engineer, and founder of an AI startup called ChatPRD. Claire now runs her business, podcast, and family life with the help of nine OpenClaw agents running on multiple Mac Minis and old laptops. In this episode, Claire shares her journey from OpenClaw skeptic (it deleted her family calendar the first time she tried it) to true believer, and gives a masterclass in using AI agents in real life.
We discuss:
1. The exact step-by-step process to install and set up OpenClaw (it’s easier than you think)
2. How to avoid the biggest OpenClaw mistakes (don’t install it on your main computer)
3. Actual use cases that have changed Claire’s life (e.g. family scheduling, inbound sales, podcast prep, and course management)
4. Why multiple specialized agents beat one general-purpose agent
5. The security risks everyone worries about—and how to handle them
6. Browser limitations, memory issues, and practical workarounds
episodes.descriptionCN:
Claire Vo 是我们的姊妹播客“How I AI”的主持人，她曾担任产品主管和工程师，也是一家名为 ChatPRD 的人工智能初创公司的创始人。 Claire 现在在多台 Mac Mini 和旧笔记本电脑上运行的 9 个 OpenClaw 代理的帮助下经营她的生意、播客和家庭生活。在这一集中，克莱尔分享了她从 OpenClaw 怀疑论者（她第一次尝试时就删除了她的家庭日历）到真正的信徒的旅程，并提供了在现实生活中使用人工智能代理的大师班。
我们讨论：
1. 安装和设置 OpenClaw 的确切分步过程（��您想象的更容易）
2. 如何避免最大的 OpenClaw 错误（不要将其安装在您的主计算机上）
3. 改变克莱尔生活的实际用例（例如家庭安排、入站销售、播客准备和课程管理）
4. 为什么多个专业代理胜过一个通用代理
5. 每个人都担心的安全风险以及如何应对
6. 浏览器限制、内存问题和实际解决方法

episodes.title: The art of influence: The single most important skill that AI can’t replace | Jessica Fain (Webflow, ex-Slack)
episodes.description:
Jessica Fain is a product leader at Webflow and former Chief of Staff to the CPO at Slack, where she worked alongside April Underwood and many past podcast guests including Stewart Butterfield, Annie Pearl, Tamar Yehoshua, and Noah Weiss. She’s spent her career learning how executives actually make decisions—and why most people completely misunderstand the process.
We discuss:
1. Why great ideas often don’t get buy-in
2. Why executive calendars are “like strobe lights” and why the first 30 seconds of a meeting matter so much
3. Why executives are usually optimizing for a global maximum while you are often optimizing locally
4. The best question Jessica uses when a leader says something that seems wrong: “That’s so interesting. What led you to believe that?”
5. Why you should go in to learn, not to convince
6. Why showing only one option is a mistake
7. Why AI will make influence more important, not less
episodes.descriptionCN:
Jessica Fain 是 Webflow 的产品负责人，也是 Slack 的前首席产品官，她与 April Underwood 以及包括 Stewart Butterfield、Annie Pearl、Tamar Yehoshua 和 Noah Weiss 在内的许多过去的播客嘉宾一起工作。她的职业生涯一直在研究高管们实际上是如何做出决策的，以及为什么大多数人完全误解了这个过程。
我们讨论：
1. 为什么伟大的想法常常得不到认可
2. 为什么高管日历“就像闪光灯”以及为什么会议的前 30 秒如此重要
3. 为什么高管通常会针对全局最大化进行优化，而你则经常进行局部优化
4. 当领导者说出看似错误的事情时，杰西卡使用的最佳问题是：“这太有趣了。是什么让你相信这一点？”
5. 为什么你应该去学习，而不是去说服
6. 为什么只显示一个选项是错误的
7. 为什么人工智能会让影响力变得更加重要，而不是更少

episodes.title: The tactical playbook for getting 20-40% more comp (without sounding greedy) | Jacob Warwick (Executive Negotiator)
episodes.description:
Jacob Warwick is an executive negotiation coach who helps senior operators negotiate better salary, equity, titles, and severance packages. He has worked with leaders across tech and Hollywood, was previously a founder and CEO himself, and has helped clients secure millions in additional compensation. His approach focuses on collaboration over confrontation, understanding motivations, and treating job searches like enterprise sales processes.
We discuss:
1. Why a simple “What’s the chance there’s a little more here?” often unlocks a 20% bump
2. Why Jacob sees 40% average movement when negotiations are run well
3. When negotiation actually starts (hint: it’s much earlier than you think)
4. Why information + timing create power
5. The biggest mistakes people make when negotiating
6. How to navigate the important “What’s your comp expectation?” question without anchoring too low
7. Why the best interviews feel more like discovery calls than interrogations
episodes.descriptionCN:
雅各布·沃里克（Jacob Warwick）是一名高管谈判教练，帮助高级经营者谈判更好的薪资、股权、头衔和遣散费。他曾与科技界和好莱坞的领导者合作，此前他本人也是创始人兼首席执行官，并帮助客户获得了数百万美元的额外报酬。他的方法侧重于合作而非对抗、理解动机以及将求职视为企业销售流程。
我们讨论：
1. 为什么是一个简单的“这里还有什么机会？”通常会解锁 20% 的提升
2. 为什么雅各布认为谈判进展顺利时平均变动率为 40%
3. 谈判何时真正开始（提示：比你想象的要早得多）
4. 为什么信息+时机创造力量
5.人们在谈判时犯的最大错误
6. 如何应对重要的“你的薪酬期望是什么？”问题没有锚定太低
7. 为什么最好的采访感觉更像是发现电话而不是审讯

episodes.title: How AI agents & Claude skills work (Clearly Explained)
episodes.description:
I sit down with Ras Mic to break down how AI agents actually work and why most people are using them wrong. Ras Mic explains the mechanics of context windows, makes the case that agent md files are largely unnecessary, and shares his step-by-step methodology for building custom skills that make agents dramatically more productive. Whether you're coding with Claude Code or automating workflows with OpenClaw, this episode gives you the foundational knowledge to stop wasting tokens and start getting real results from your AI tools.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
00:42 – The Models Are Good Now
01:20 – How Context Windows Actually Work
04:55 – The Power of Skills
09:17 – How to create Skills
16:35 – Skill Maxxing
19:05 – What you need too build a project
20:40 – Recursively Building and Improving Skills
29:23 – Context Window Management and Token Efficiency
33:02 – Closing Thoughts
Key Points
* The models (Opus 4.6, GPT 5.4) are exceptionally good now — the differentiator is the context and harness you build around them.
* Agent md and claude md files get loaded into context on every single turn, burning tokens and degrading performance as the context window fills up. 95% of users can skip them entirely.
* Skills use progressive disclosure: only the name and description sit in context until the agent determines it needs the full file, saving thousands of tokens per conversation.
* The best way to create a skill is to walk through the workflow with the agent step by step, achieve a successful run, and then have the agent write the skill based on that real context.
* Recursively refine skills by feeding failures back into the agent and having it update the skill file so the same mistake is avoided going forward.
* Scale for productivity by starting with one agent and building up workflows before adding sub-agents — start simple, then expand.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Models Are Good — Context Is What Matters
Ras Mic opens by declaring that the current generation of models, Opus 4.6 and GPT 5.4, are exceptionally capable. The conversation is no longer about which model is "better" in a general sense. What matters now is the quality of context you feed them — that is what separates quality output from slop.
2. How Context Windows Work
Ras Mic walks through the anatomy of a context window: system prompt, agent.md files, skills, tools, the codebase, and the user conversation. All of these stack up as tokens, and the window has a hard limit (around 250,000 tokens). When you hit that limit, agents compact — and performance drops. Understanding this structure is the foundation for everything else in the episode.
3. Skills and Progressive Disclosure
Skills solve the token-bloat problem. A skill file contains a name, description, and the detailed instructions — but only the name and description are loaded into context. The agent reads the full file only when it determines the skill is relevant. This means a skill costs roughly 53 tokens per turn versus 944+ for an equivalent agent.md file.
4. Building Skills the Right Way
Ras Mic shares his methodology: identify a workflow, walk through it with the agent step by step, correct mistakes in real time, and only create the skill after you have completed a successful run. He illustrates this with his sponsor email screening agent — the first attempt returned all-positive results because the agent had no criteria for rejection.
5. Recursively Improving Skills
Even after a skill is created, the agent will still hit edge cases and fail. Ras Mic treats each failure as an opportunity: identify the error, have the agent fix it, then tell the agent to update the skill so the failure is documented. After five iterations of this loop on his YouTube analytics report generator, the agent now executes flawlessly across eight data sources in about ten minutes.
6. Scaling for Productivity Over Flash
Ras Mic started with a single agent handling everything — email, spreadsheets, research. Only after building reliable skills did he add sub-agents for marketing, business, and personal tasks. He argues that jumping straight to multi-agent architectures (or adopting tools like Paperclip without building foundational workflows first) optimizes for what looks cool rather than what is productive.

episodes.descriptionCN:
我和 Ras Mic 一起坐下来分析人工智能代理的实际工作原理以及为什么大多数人都错误地使用它们。 Ras Mic 解释了上下文窗口的机制，说明了代理 md 文件在很大程度上是不必要的，并分享了他构建自定义技能的分步方法，这些技能使代理显着提高了工作效率。无论您是使用 Claude Code 进行编码，还是使用 OpenClaw 实现工作流程自动化，本集都会为您提供基础知识，帮助您停止浪费代币并开始从 AI 工具中获得真正的结果。
时间戳
00:00 – 简介
00:42 – 模型现在很好
01:20 – 上下文窗口实际上是如何工作的
04:55 – 技能的力量
09:17 – 如何创造技能
16:35 – 技能最大化
19:05 – 构建项目还需要什么
20:40 – 递归地建立和提高技能
29:23 – 上下文窗口管理和代币效率
33:02 – 结束语
要点
* 模型（Opus 4.6、GPT 5.4）现在非常好——区别在于您围绕它们构建的环境和工具。
* Agent md 和 claude md 文件在每一回合都会加载到上下文中，随着上下文窗口填满，会燃烧令牌并降低性能。 95% 的用户可以完全跳过它们。
* 技能使用渐进式披露：只有名称和描述位于上下文中，直到代理确定需要完整文件，从而在每次对话中节省数千个令牌。
* 创建技能的最佳方法是与代理一起逐步完成工作流程，实现成功运行，然后让代理根据真实上下文编写技能。
* 通过将失败反馈给代理并让它更新技能文件来递归地完善技能，从而避免今后出现同样的错误。
* 通过从一个代理开始并在添加子代理之前建立工作流程来扩展生产力 - 从简单开始，然后扩展。
编号部分摘要
1. 模型很好——背景才是��重要的
Ras Mic 首先宣称当前一代的型号 Opus 4.6 和 GPT 5.4 功能异常强大。讨论不再是一般意义上哪种模型“更好”。现在重要的是你为他们提供的上下文的质量——这就是高质量输出和废品的区别。
2. 上下文窗口如何工作
Ras Mic 详细剖析了上下文窗口：系统提示符、agent.md 文件、技能、工具、代码库和用户对话。所有这些都作为代币堆积起来，并且窗口有一个硬性限制（大约 250,000 个代币）。当达到该限制时，代理会紧凑，并且性能会下降。理解这个结构是本集中其他所有内容的基础。
3. 技能和渐进式披露
技能解决了令牌膨胀问题。技能文件包含名称、描述和详细说明，但只有名称和描述会加载到上下文中。仅当代理确定技能相关时才会读取完整文件��这意味着一项技能每回合大约花费 53 个代币，而同等的 agent.md 文件则需要 944+ 个代币。
4. 以正确的方式培养技能
Ras Mic 分享了他的方法论：确定工作流程，与代理一起逐步执行该工作流程，实时纠正错误，并且仅在成功完成运行后才创建技能。他用他的赞助商电子邮件筛选代理来说明这一点——第一次尝试返回了全阳性结果，因为代理没有拒绝标准。
5. 递归地提高技能
即使创建了技能后，代理仍然会遇到边缘情况并失败。 Ras Mic 将每次失败视为一次机会：识别错误，让代理修复它，然后告诉代理更新技能，以便记录失败。在 YouTube 分析报告生成器上循环执行五次之后，该代理现在可以在大约十分钟内完美地跨八个数据源执行。
6. 通过闪存扩展生产力
Ras Mic 最初由一个代理处理所有事务——电子邮件、电子表格、研究。只有在建立了可靠的技能之后，他才添加了负责营销、业务和个人任务的子代理。他认为，直接跳到多代理架构（或采用 Paperclip 等工具而不首先构建基础工作流程）会优化看起来很酷的东西，而不是高效的东西。

episodes.title: How to turn iMessage into an AI executive assistant with Lindy
episodes.description:
I sit down with Flo, founder of Lindy, to get a live demo of their new product, Lindy Assistant, an AI executive assistant that lives in iMessage and works proactively across email, calendar, Slack, Notion, and 100-plus other tools. Flo walks me through a real day of his own Lindy usage, showing how it drafts email replies, prepares meeting briefs, updates CRMs, and handles calendar changes without being asked. We compare Lindy to OpenClaw and Claude's ecosystem, talk pricing, edge-case power users, and where Lindy goes over the next five years.
Try the ultimate AI assistant:
Key Points
* Lindy Assistant lives in iMessage, connects to email, calendar, Slack, Notion, and 100-plus other apps, and acts proactively without being prompted.
* Setup takes two minutes: provide a phone number and connect a Google account, and Lindy ingests existing email and tool data immediately.
* Lindy pre-drafts email replies, preps meeting briefs, updates CRMs after calls, flags billing issues, and reschedules dinners at closed restaurants — all without user initiation.
* The voice and tone of the assistant took extensive prompt engineering; the lowercase, casual register is intentional and difficult to achieve with current models.
* Lindy targets the "chief everything officer" — the overwhelmed founder or executive — rather than developers or power users who want a fully programmable agent.
* Pricing starts at $49/month for 90-plus percent of users; heavy users can exceed that and are prompted to upgrade.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Morning Brief and Proactive Email Triage Each morning, Lindy sends a summary over iMessage: weather, meetings on the calendar, a count of overnight emails triaged, and pre-drafted replies. Flo demonstrates a real example where Lindy caught a restaurant closure, proposed a nearby alternative, and confirmed a meeting with Joshua — all before Flo opened Gmail or his calendar.
2. Human-Sounding Tone as a Product Differentiator The lowercase, conversational register, including the occasional profanity when something goes wrong — required significant prompt engineering. Flo notes that model defaults are effectively baked into model weights, making it genuinely hard to get consistent tonal results. This attention to voice is one of Lindy's clearest differentiators from generic AI chat tools.
3. Live Meeting Intelligence and Post-Meeting Actions During a live in-meeting demo, Flo shows Lindy sending a summary to a teammate who was absent, creating a Google Doc of failure modes discussed, and posting it to Slack, all triggered by a quick iMessage during the meeting itself.
4. Research, Scheduling, and Sales — Covering My Three Use Cases I walk through the three things my current human assistant handles: research, scheduling, and inbound sales lead follow-up. Flo maps each directly to Lindy capabilities — pre-meeting research briefs pull from the public web and private meeting history; scheduling finds mutual availability and sends invites; and CRM integrations mean inbound leads can trigger immediate, context-aware outreach.
5. Lindy vs. OpenClaw vs. Claude Flo frames OpenClaw as Linux — extremely powerful, self-modifying, and suited to technical users comfortable with the risk. He frames Claude as Android, powerful and horizontal but requiring significant configuration. Lindy is the iPhone: opinionated, polished, built for people who want results on day one without devoting a weekend to setup. The target user is a real estate agent, a sports bar owner, or a roofing contractor.

我与 Lindy 的创始人 Flo 坐下来，现场演示了他们的新产品 Lindy Assistant，这是一个人工智能行政助理，位于 iMessage 中，可以在电子邮件、日历、Slack、Notion 和 100 多种其他工具中主动工作。 Flo 向我介绍了他自己使用 Lindy 的真实情况，展示了如何在没有询问的情况下起草电子邮件回复、准备会议简报、更新 CRM 以及处理日历更改。我们将 Lindy 与 OpenClaw 和 Claude 的生态系统、定价、边缘情况高级用户以及 Lindy 未来五年的发展方向进行比较。
尝试终极人工智能助手：
要点
* Lindy Assistant 位于 iMessage 中，可连接到电子邮件、日历、Slack、Notion 和 100 多个其他应用程序，并在没有提示的情况下主动采取行动。
* 设置需要两分钟：提供电话号码并连接 Google 帐户，Lindy 会立即提取现有电子邮件和工具数据。
* Lindy 预先起草电子邮件回复、准备会议简报、在通话后更新 CRM、标记计费问题以及在已关闭的餐厅重新安排晚餐 - 所有这些都无需用户启动。
* 助理的声音和语气经过了大量的提示工程；小写、随意的寄存器是有意为之的，并且当前模型很难实现。
* Lindy 的目标是“首席事务官”——不知所措的创始人或高管——而不是想要完全可编程代理的开发人员或高级用户。
* 90% 以上的用户的定价为 49 美元/月；重度用户可能会超出此范围，并会提示升级。
编号部分摘要
1. 早间简报和主动电子邮件分类 每天早上，Lindy 通过 iMessage 发送摘要：天气、日历上的会议、隔夜分类的电子邮件计数以及预先起草的回复。 Flo 演示了一个真实的例子，Lindy 发现餐厅关门了，提出了附近的替代方案，并确认了与 Joshua 的会面 - 所有这些都在 Flo 打开 Gmail 或他的日历之前完成。
2. 人性化的语气作为产品的差异化因素小写的对话语体，包括出现问题时偶尔出现的脏话，需要大量的提示工程。 Flo 指出，模型默认值实际上已融入模型权重中，因此很难获得一致的色调结果。对语音的关注是 Lindy 与通用人工智能聊天工具最明显的区别之一。
3. 实时会议情报和会后操作 在实时会议演示中，Flo 展示了 Lindy 向缺席的队友发送摘要、创建讨论的故障模式的 Google 文档，并将其发布到 Slack，所有这些都是由会议期间的快速 iMessage 触发的。
4. 研究、日程安排和销售 - 涵盖我的三个用例我会介绍我当前的人工助理处理的三件事：研究、日程安排和入站销售线索跟进。 Flo 将每个功能直接映射到 Lindy 功能 - 从公共网络和私人会议历史记录中提取的会前研究简报；调度发现相互可用性并发送邀请； CRM 集成意味着入站销售线索可以触发即时、情境感知的外展。
5. Lindy vs. OpenClaw vs. Claude Flo 将 OpenClaw 定义为 Linux — 极其强大、可自我修改，并且适合能够承受风险的技术用户。他将 Claude 描述为 Android，功能强大且横向，但需要大量配置。 Lindy 就是 iPhone：固执己见、精致，专为那些希望第一天就得到结果而不需要花一个周末进行设置的人而设计。目标用户是房地产经纪人、体育酒吧老板或屋顶承包商。

episodes.title: 23 AI Trends keeping me up at night
episodes.description:
I go solo on this episode to walk through the full list of AI trends and opportunities keeping me up at night — literally. From the one-hour company stack to ambient businesses, vertical AI, the agent economy, and the real security threats I see coming, I cover what I believe is the most asymmetric window in startup history. I share the frameworks I use to think about what to build, what to avoid, and why acting now matters more than waiting for things to settle down.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
01:09 – 1) The One-Hour Company Stack
02:09 – 2) Old vs. new startup timeline
03:58 – 3) Ambient businesses and autonomous companies
05:18 – 4) The agent economy timeline
07:17 – 5) Agent hiring Agents
08:01 – 6) The Vertical Agent Map
09:39 – 7) Vertical AI vs. Vertical SaaS
10:53 – 8) Boring goldmine verticals
11:40 – 9) SaaS Pricing Evolution
13:26 – 10) Seat-Based vs Outcome-Based
14:51 – 11) The SaaS graveyard
16:04 – 12) The scarcity flip
17:03 – 13) The Premium Stack
18:21 – 14) The experience economy boom
18:59 – 15) Founder-agent fit
20:32 – 16) Ghost team org chart
21:56 – 17) The micro monopoly math
24:00 – 18) Agent attack surface
25:19 – 19) Agent Injection vs Phishing
26:34 – 20) Agent permission stack
27:37 – 21) The closing window
28:46 – 22) why this window is asymmetric
29:34 – 23) Building in public
30:50 – Final Thoughts
Key Points
* I can build, launch, and get a first customer in under an hour using today's agent engineering tools and a pre-existing audience.
* Vertical AI taps directly into labor P&L — it replaces headcount, not just software licenses — making the TAM 10x larger than vertical SaaS.
* Ambient businesses running on near-zero daily human input are early but real; the arrow of progress points here.
* The value shift I see coming: execution gets commoditized, judgment and physical presence become premium.
* The 100 true fans model now applies in the AI age; with agents cutting costs, 100 paying customers at $500–$1,000 a month builds a real business.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The One-Hour Company Stack I walk through how grabbing a validated idea from a tool like IdeaBrowser, vibe coding an MVP, adding Stripe, and reaching first customers now fits inside a single morning. The old 12-month runway to first revenue is gone. The constraint today is distribution, not development — which is why I keep emphasizing building an audience before you need it.
2. Ambient and Autonomous Businesses I introduce the concept of ambient businesses — operations that run with zero or very low daily human input, where agents monitor markets, identify opportunities, handle customer service, and execute. I believe these businesses will reach seven and eight figures. Most autonomous builder software today produces AI slop, but the direction is clear and the opportunity is real.
3. The Agent Economy Timeline I map three distinct eras: the App Store era (2009–2015), the API economy (2015–2024), and the agent economy I see running from 2025 to 2030. In this era, agents discover and hire other agents on the fly. I flag a startup idea I can't stop thinking about: a Glassdoor for AI agents, a reputation layer for an agent marketplace. Gartner puts 20% of commerce as machine-to-machine by 2030.
4. Founder-Agent Fit and the Ghost Team Founder-market fit is being replaced by founder-agent fit — the ability to orchestrate a fleet of agents toward a goal, the way a film director gets performances out of actors. I describe the ghost team org chart: a two-person company with a full suite of named AI agents running sales, content, and support. As a person building a holding company, I think this model makes owning multiple AI-native businesses in adjacent niches very achievable.
5. Micro Monopoly Math and the 100 True Fans Update Kevin Kelly's thousand true fans model shrinks to 100 in the AI age because agents cut operating costs so dramatically. I run the math: a 5,000-person niche audience, a custom app built in 48 hours, 100 customers at $50/month equals roughly $60,000 in profit for one person. Stack several of these and you have a holding company. The bottleneck is distribution, which is why building media and understanding paid acquisition matters.

在这一集里，我将独自回顾人工智能趋势和机会的完整列表，让我彻夜难眠——字面上的意思。从一小时的公司堆栈到环境业务、垂直人工智能、代理经济以及我看到的真正的安全威胁，我涵盖了我认为是初创公司历史上最不对称的窗口。我分享了我用来思考要构建什么、要避免什么以及为什么现在采取行动比等待事情稳定下来更重要的框架。

要点
* 我可以使用当今的代理工程工具和预先存在的受众在一小时内建立、启动并获得第一个客户。
* 垂直人工智能直接利用劳动力损益——它取代了员工数量，而不仅仅是软件许可证——使得 TAM 比垂直 SaaS 大 10 倍。
* 日常人力投入几乎为零的环境企业虽然很早，但确实存在；进步的箭头指向这里。
* 我认为价值的转变即将到来：执行力变得商品化，判断力和实际存在变得溢价。
* 100个真粉丝模型现在适用于AI时代；通过代理商削减成本，100 个每月支付 500 至 1,000 美元的付费客户可以建立起真正的业务。
编号部分摘要
1. 一小时公司堆栈 我将介绍如何从 IdeaBrowser 等工具中获取经过验证的想法、对 MVP 进行氛围编码、添加 Stripe，以及如何在一个早上内接触到第一批客户。过去 12 个月才能获得第一笔收入的做法已经不复存在。今天的限制是发行，而不是开发——这就是为什么我一直强调在需要之前建立受众。
2. 环境和自主业务我介绍了环境业务的概念——日常人力投入为零或非常低的运营，代理监控市场、发现机会、处理客户服务并执行。我相信这些业务将会达到七、八位数。如今，大多数自主构建软件都会产生人工智能溢出，但方向是明确的，机会是真实的。
3. 代理经济时间表 我绘制了三个不同的时代：App Store 时代（2009-2015）、API 经济（2015-2024）以及我认为从 2025 年到 2030 年的代理经济。在这个时代，代理会不断发现并雇佣其他代理。我提出了一个我无法停止思考的创业想法：人工智能代理的 Glassdoor，代理市场的声誉层。 Gartner 预计，到 2030 年，20% 的商业将通过机器对机器进行。
4. 创始人-经纪人契合度和幽灵团队 创始人-市场契合度正在被创始人-经纪人契合度所取代——即协调一批经纪人实现目标的能力，就像电影导演让演员表演的方式一样。我描述了幽灵团队的组织结构图：一家两人公司，拥有一整套指定的人工智能代理，负责销售、内容和支持。作为一个创办控股公司的人，我认为这种模式使得在相邻领域拥有多个人工智能原生企业变得非常容易实现。
5. 微观垄断数学和 100 个忠实粉丝更新 在人工智能时代，凯文·凯利 (Kevin Kelly) 的千名忠实粉丝模型缩减至 100 个，因为代理商大幅削减了运营成本。我计算了一下：一个 5,000 人的利基受众，一个在 48 小时内构建的定制应用程序，100 名客户，每月 50 美元，相当于一个人的利润大约为 60,000 美元。将其中的几个叠加在一起，您就拥有了一家控股公司。瓶颈在于分发，这就是为什么建立媒体和了解付费获取很重要。

episodes.title: How to 10x your Claude with 4 .md files
episodes.description:
I sit down with Remy Gaskell to break down how anyone can build AI agents to run entire departments of their business. Remy walks through the core concepts: agent loops, context files, memory, MCP tool connections, and skills. We put everything together by building a fully functional executive assistant live on screen. This is a beginner-friendly crash course that covers Claude Code, Codex, Cowork, Antigravity, Manus, and OpenClaw, showing that once you understand how to "drive," you can jump into any agent platform. By the end, listeners know exactly how to set up markdown-based context files, connect their everyday tools, and create reusable skills that compound over weeks and months.
Key Points
* Agent platforms (Claude Code, Codex, Cowork, Antigravity, Manus, OpenClaw) are all running the same observe-think-act loop under the hood — learning one means you can use any of them.
* The shift from chat to agents requires moving from prompt engineering to context engineering: load the agent with rich context so simple prompts produce excellent results.
* A memory md file creates a self-improving loop where the agent learns preferences across sessions and makes fewer errors over time.
* MCP (Model Context Protocol), built by Anthropic, acts as a universal translator between your agent and every tool it needs — Gmail, Calendar, Stripe, Notion, and more.
* Skills are reusable SOPs packaged as markdown files; once you explain a process once, you can invoke it repeatedly, and they compound as you add three to five per week.
* Scheduled tasks turn skills into automated workflows — morning briefs, car searches, ad library analyses — that run on a cron without any manual trigger.
Numbered Section Summaries
1. The Agent Loop in Action
Remy kicks off with a live demo, sending the same prompt — "build a minimalist portfolio site for Greg Isenberg" — to Claude Code, Codex, and Antigravity simultaneously. All three platforms run the same observe-think-act loop: research the subject, write the code, spin up a preview, and verify the result with a screenshot. The demo makes it tangible that every agent harness is just a different car with the same engine.
2. Onboarding Your Agent Like a Real Employee
Remy shows that without context, an agent asked to "write me a cold email" has no idea who you are or what you sell. The fix is an agents.md (or Claude.md) file — a persistent context document loaded at the start of every session. You fill it with your role, business details, tools, and working preferences, and the result is that a two-word prompt produces a fully informed output.
3. Memory That Compounds
Chat models store memory invisibly in the cloud; agents require you to build it intentionally. Remy adds a memory.md file and a simple instruction in the context file: "When I correct you or you learn something new, update memory.md." Preferences like tone, email sign-offs, and design choices persist across sessions, and errors decrease over time.

我与 Remy Gaskell 坐下来探讨了如何构建人工智能代理来运行其整个业务部门。 Remy 介绍了核心概念：代理循环、上下文文件、内存、MCP 工具连接和技能。我们通过在屏幕上实时构建功能齐全的行政助理来将所有内容整合在一起。这是一门适合初学者的速成课程，涵盖 Claude Code、Codex、Cowork、Antigravity、Manus 和 OpenClaw，表明一旦您了解如何“驾驶”，您就可以跳入任何代理平台。到最后，听众将确切地知道如何设置基于 Markdown 的上下文文件、连接日常工具以及创建可在数周和数月内复合的可重用技能。
要点
* 代理平台（Claude Code、Codex、Cowork、Antigravity、Manus、OpenClaw）都在幕后运行相同的观察-思考-行动循环 - 学习一个意味着您可以使用其中任何一个。
* 从聊天到代理的转变需要从提示工程转向上下文工程：为代理加载丰富的上下文，以便简单的提示产生出色的结果。
* 内存 md 文件创建了一个自我改进的循环，代理可以在会话中学习偏好，并随着时间的推移减少错误。
* MCP（模型上下文协议）由 Anthropic 构建，充当您的代理与其所需的每个工具（Gmail、日历、Stripe、Notion 等）之间的通用转换器。
* 技能是打包为markdown文件的可重复使用的SOP；一旦你解释了一个过程一次，你就可以重复调用它，并且当你每周添加三到五个时，它们就会复合。
* 计划任务将技能转化为自动化工作流程——早间简报、汽车搜索、广告库分析——在 cron 上运行，无需任何手动触发。
编号部分摘要
1. 代理循环的实际应用
Remy 以现场演示开始，同时向 Claude Code、Codex 和 Antigravity 发送相同的提示——“为 Greg Isenberg 构建一个极简主义作品集网站”。所有三个平台都运行相同的观察-思考-行动循环：研究主题、编写代码、启动预览，并使用屏幕截图验证结果。该演示清楚地表明，每个代理线束只是一辆具有相同发动机的不同汽车。
2. 像真正的员工一样培训您的代理
雷米表明，在没有背景的情况下，要求“给我写一封冷冰冰的电子邮件”的代理商不知道你是谁或你卖什么。修复方法是使用agents.md（或Claude.md）文件——一个在每个会话开始时加载的持久上下文文档。您可以在其中填写您的角色、业务详细信息、工具和工作偏好，结果是两个词的提示会产生完全知情的输出。
3. 复合记忆
聊天模型将内存无形地存储在云端；代理要求您有意构建它。 Remy 在上下文文件中添加了一个 memory.md 文件和一条简单的指令：“当我纠正你或者你学到新东西时，更新 memory.md。”语气、电子邮件结束和设计选择等偏好在整个会话中持续存在，并且错误会随着时间的推移而减少。

episodes.title: Stop Vibe Coding. Start Getting Customers.
episodes.description:
I break down the seven distribution strategies every vibe coder and builder needs to actually get customers. With 200,000 new projects launching daily on platforms like Lovable, the real bottleneck is distribution and I believe the wealthiest people over the next decade will be marketers, because code is now commoditized. I walk through each strategy with step-by-step instructions you can start this week, from MCP servers and programmatic SEO to acquiring newsletters and building AI repurposing engines.
Timestamps
00:00 – Intro
01:07 – The Great Flip: Distribution Over Engineering
03:08 – The Build-First Trap
04:18 – Strategy 1: MCP Servers as Your Sales Team
06:49 – Strategy 2: Programmatic SEO (10,000 Pages)
10:09 – Strategy 3: Free Tool as Top of Funnel
13:03 – Strategy 4: Answer Engine Optimization (AEO)
15:48 – Strategy 5: Viral Artifacts (Make Outputs Shareable)
18:56 – Strategy 6: Buy a Niche Newsletter
21:40 – Strategy 7: AI Content Repurposing Engine
25:13 – Final Takeaways
Key Points
* Distribution is the new moat — AI can build the product, but it can't build your audience or brand.
* Building an MCP server in 2026 is like building for mobile in 2010; early movers will own AI-native distribution channels.
* Programmatic SEO can scale to 300,000 monthly visitors if you create 10,000 quality pages that each pull just 30 visits a month.
* Free tools act as always-on marketing: you can vibe code one in a day, ship it by lunch, and it markets itself forever.
* Answer engine optimization (AEO) is where SEO was in 2010 — Peter Levels saw AI referrals jump from 4% to 20% in one month.
* You can buy a 10,000-subscriber niche newsletter for $5,000–$20,000 and inherit a direct channel to your exact audience on day one.
Numbered Section Summaries
Strategy 1 — MCP Servers as Your AI Sales Team
I walk through how building an MCP server lets AI assistants like Claude and ChatGPT discover and serve your product automatically — zero customer acquisition cost. A friend in fintech got 150+ installations in 30 days with $0 ad spend. I share the steps: identify the question your product answers, build the MCP server in under 24 hours, and publish to registries like Smithery, MCPT, and OpenTools.
Strategy 2 — Programmatic SEO at Scale
I lay out the math: 10,000 pages × 30 visits each = 300,000 monthly visitors; at a 2% conversion rate and $10 per conversion, that's $60,000/month from pages you build once. The process starts with a keyword pattern like "best X for Y," structured data via tools like Firecrawl, a Next.js page template, and AI-generated content — with a human in the loop to keep quality high. I recommend publishing 100 pages as an MVP before scaling.
Strategies 3 & 4 — Free Tools and Answer Engine Optimization
I cover the free-tool-as-marketing playbook (using Ahrefs' backlink checker as the classic example) and explain why AI makes it possible to ship a free tool every week. Then I introduce answer engine optimization — getting cited by ChatGPT and Perplexity through structured FAQ content, schema markup, and comparison tables. I lay out how to Google the top 20 questions your customer asks and write definitive, citation-worthy answers for each.
Strategy 5 — Viral Artifacts (Make Your Output Shareable)
I break down why Spotify Wrapped gets 100 million shares every December and how you can apply the same psychology: identify what your user wants to brag about and make that artifact beautiful, branded, and easy to share. I point to GitHub's contribution graph, Stripe Atlas incorporation milestones, and Duolingo streaks as examples. This works in B2B too — people share within Slack and Teams all the time.
Strategy 6 — AI Content Repurposing Engine
I explain the "one pillar, many channels" approach: record one 30-minute piece of content (podcast, video, or voice memo), then use AI to turn it into 5–10 tweets, 3–5 LinkedIn posts, 2–3 short-form videos, a newsletter, a blog post, and 5–10 quote graphics. I recommend starting by just recording and transcribing, then feeding that into Claude. If you do this every week, in three months you'll have more content than your competitors.
